data.title: Credo: Time To Admit I Was Dead Wrong (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The connectivity layer is becoming one of the most critical bottlenecks amid the AI revolution, and I underestimated this structural factor when I initiated Credo (CRDO) coverage with a bearish opinion. In the reality of a rapidly expanding addressable market, competition risk that I cited in my previous write-up is becoming less significant because the market is becoming so big and there is room for growth for several players. Moreover, CRDO is not just capitalizing on data center tailwinds but also innovating aggressively to position itself as a vital vendor in the AI era. The company's business model proved itself extremely efficient, as CRDO's profitability is expanding aggressively alongside soaring revenue growth. With strong revenue and EPS growth momentum fortified by accelerating AI tailwinds, we are likely to see dramatic multiple contraction over the next few years.

Fundamental analysis

My previous CRDO article was published on May 13 when CRDO traded at $59. The share price has increased by 72% since then, meaning that I was dead wrong with my bearish call. When analyzing stocks, I like examining how earnings revisions changed over time. In the below table, I am comparing consensus EPS projections for Credo as of today against as of mid-May 2025. We can see that positive earnings revisions outpaced share price growth significantly across the board. It implies that Credo's share price growth since my previous coverage was pretty modest compared to soaring earnings revisions.

The company delivered 200%+ year-over-year revenue growth over the last three quarters and 150%+ growth over the last five quarters. Meanwhile, Credo's EPS demonstrated even more aggressive growth. For example, last quarter's revenue increased by 201%, while the EPS increased by 328% year-over-year. If we refer to Credo's income statement over the last several quarters, we can see that the company's gross and operating margins have been expanding sequentially over the last several quarters. Therefore, Credo's operating leverage and pricing power are structurally strong. It means that we are likely to see further profitability ratios expansion if aggressive revenue growth continues.

And I believe that aggressive revenue growth is likely to last for longer. Of course, Credo's rapidly growing sales are thanks to unprecedented AI infrastructure tailwinds as hyperscalers are aggressively ramping up data center buildout. Besides structural AI infrastructure tailwinds, I also have to give credit to the company's commitment to innovation. It is vital because it helps to deepen the company's technology moat versus competitors. Credo recently introduced its Robin 800G optical DSP family tailored for the next generation of AI clusters. The new product's aim is to capture market share in the rapidly growing 800G optical transceivers niche because Robin offers a compact, low-power DSP built-in. And the company is building its 800G AI optics moat methodically. Besides the Robin release, the company also launched the 800G ZeroFlap Optical Transceiver. It implies a move to vertical integration because it shifts Credo up into full optical modules instead of being purely a DSP/AEC vendor. Having multiple products that complement each other creates the potential to build ecosystem lock-in.

Finally, it is also important to mention that CRDO currently trades at just above $100. Over the past few months, this milestone proved to be a strong support level. The current RSI level of 39 also appears attractive because CRDO is currently not far above the oversold territory. Therefore, CRDO looks really good from the technical analysis perspective as well.

Valuation analysis

As I mentioned earlier, Credo's positive earnings revisions by far outpaced share price growth since my previous coverage. It implies that the stock witnessed a significant P/E ratio compression. In my fundamental analysis, I explained why I believe CRDO is able to maintain aggressive earnings growth for longer.

Therefore, I believe that Wall Street's forward P/E projection with dramatic multiple compression is reliable. Analysts expect substantial earnings growth rate deceleration after FY2026, which is absolutely reasonable considering the rule of large numbers. The estimated FY2027 forward P/E ratio of 21.5 looks modest, especially taking into account that we are speaking about fiscal year and not calendar year. Credo's next reportable quarter is Q4 FY2026, meaning that FY2027 commencement is around the corner.

Besides Credo's exponential revenue and EPS growth, I believe that the company's rock-solid financial position also supports the undervaluation assertion. The company ended the previous quarter with $1.3 billion in cash and only $16 million in total debt. It is important from a valuation perspective because a clean balance sheet provides any company with the optionality to invest in multiple growth projects simultaneously, which increases the chances of maintaining aggressive earnings growth for longer.

Mitigating factors

The stock market is a voting machine over the short term, meaning that sentiment prevails over fundamentals. The macro environment is currently quite cloudy, and some fears look legit. The war in the Gulf makes investors nervous about energy supply disruptions, which results in soaring oil and gas prices. Since almost every product and service has energy in its cost structure, increasing prices for hydrocarbons means upward pressure on inflation. In turn, higher inflation makes the Fed more hawkish, while at the end of 2025 investors were expecting this year to be another year of interest rate cuts. With soaring energy prices, rate cuts appear to be off the table. Given the fact that a few of the Gulf's large oil and gas production and processing facilities have already suffered from drone strikes, the global energy supply chain is indeed under threat. Therefore, market sentiment might continue deteriorating, which will weigh on valuations of all growth stocks.

Credo is winning new contracts and is innovating aggressively to diversify its revenue mix. However, customer concentration risk remains significant. According to the fresh 10-Q report, two customers account for more than 80% of Credo's sales. Heavy dependence on just two customers is a risk because one of the largest customers might pause or introduce vendor diversification, which will undermine Credo's revenue growth potential. Moreover, having significant dependence on just a few large customers for longer will likely depreciate Credo's pricing power over time once the AI connectivity bottleneck eases.

Conclusion

I was all wrong about Credo when I initiated coverage in May. The company's scale was quite small back then, and I thought it would be really difficult for them to compete against giants like Broadcom and Marvell. However, despite there being several rapidly growing players in the AI connectivity domain, it remains a critical bottleneck. In such an environment, CRDO has structural pricing power because demand for its offerings is really strong. Moreover, the company isn't just resting on its laurels but also ramps up innovation quite aggressively. Credo's positive earnings revisions far outpaced share price growth since May, meaning that the forward P/E ratio setup has become extremely bullish. With all that being said, I upgrade CRDO to 'Buy.'

data.title: From AI To Consumer Spending: 5 Analyst Days Investors Should Track
data.description:
We are already a few years into 2026.

It sure feels that way, right? An onslaught of macro events, geopolitical shocks, and multi-standard-deviation moves within some sub-asset classes has left investors wondering where and when the next major upheaval will strike.

In truth, we are just 11 weeks into the year, and quarter-end is not far off. The April–May stretch is when shareholder meeting season kicks into overdrive—we’ll be sure to cover the notable gatherings and detail what they mean for investors and the economy.

April-May is Peak Shareholder Meeting Season

What Are Analyst Days?

In the here and now, Analyst Days take center stage. This corporate event type differs from annual general meetings in a few key ways:

First, Analyst Days (also called Investor Days) are often voluntary marketing and educational events that aim to detail a company’s long-term strategy, product pipeline, and firm-specific trends. Reading between the lines, a company likely wouldn’t host an Investor Day if it had bad news to share. Conversely, shareholder meetings are typically mandated and focus on governance.

Next, Analyst Days are designed for both Wall Street sell-side research analysts and large existing or prospective buy-side institutional investors. In contrast, shareholder meetings are open to stockholders only (including retail investors).

Furthermore, Analyst Days call upon a company’s segment leaders (not necessarily the CEO) to showcase bench strength, serving as an extension of guidance offered within earnings presentations and quarterly conference calls. Shareholder meetings tend to be more backward-looking and may even include an airing of grievances (so to speak) among shareholders. There could even be answers to M&A questions for added volatility fuel.

NVIDIA (NVDA) GTC AI was the show-stopper Analyst Day of Q1, but you should mark a handful more events on your volatility calendar:

Generac (GNRC) — Wednesday, March 25

AI and data centers are the theme as Q1 comes to a close. Generac hosts its Investor Day in a couple of weeks, and the tone should be upbeat—shares of the $12 billion market-cap Industrials sector company are up almost 50% on the year. According to the firm, Aaron Jagdfeld, President and CEO, and other members of the company’s management team will discuss key strategic priorities and growth opportunities.

Data center construction and power generation could be front and center, as Generac is a leading global designer and manufacturer of energy technology solutions and other power products. While not an official objective, the Wisconsin-based household name has been rebranding itself more toward data center infrastructure and less about at-home portable generators. GNRC soared 18% after an upbeat Q1 report last month.

Quanta Services (PWR) — Tuesday, March 31

A trio of Analyst Days occurs on the final day of the month. Quanta Services presents live from New York City, including a Q&A session focused on the company’s strategic initiatives and new long-term financial targets. Like GNRC, PWR has posted a very strong start to 2026, and it sees 20% adjusted EPS growth this year. On the fourth quarter earnings call, the management team noted a record $44 billion backlog. While many other AI darlings have sold off recently, Quanta shares hover near all-time highs.

Amid the pomp and circumstance, executives will hopefully address key questions, including the latest economy-wide AI spending trends and the rising costs households are bearing.

Constellation Energy (CEG) — Tuesday, March 31

Constellation is one of those AI names that has pulled back sharply from all-time highs. CEG topped out at $412 last October and remains about 25% below that peak. Now under the 200-day moving average (along with a bearish “death cross,” as pointed out by technical traders), froth has clearly been wiped off the narrative.

But can CEO Joseph Dominguez flip the script? We’ll know more on the morning of March 31. Few details have been shared about the 2026 Business and Earnings Outlook event, though it’s reasonable to conclude that the market’s response to the Analyst Day could shape the ensuing Q1 earnings season.

Consider its size: Constellation is a $109 billion market-cap behemoth in the suddenly outperforming Utilities sector. It has partnered with major AI hyperscalers—Wall Street once lauded those new deals, but they are now arguably more in question.

The Hershey Company (HSY) — Tuesday, March 31

Turning from AI to the consumer, the quarter wraps up with a snack of sorts. The Hershey Company’s 2026 Investor Day occurs as the stock is up a solid 21% YTD. Offering a sweet 2.7% dividend yield, shares may appeal to a variety of investors. There are many moving pieces, including the ongoing impact of GLP-1 weight loss drugs, the rise and fall of cocoa prices, and the broader state of household spending.

Still, on its Q4 earnings call last month, the Consumer Staples brand name called for up to 400 basis points of gross margin recovery this year, along with an expanded innovation pipeline. It all sounds delicious, and a recent dividend hike is icing on the cake (or chocolate bar!).

FedEx (FDX) — Wednesday, April 8

We finish with FedEx. Its fiscal Q3 2026 revenue and earnings cross the wires this Thursday after the market close. Among the most pivotal off-season reporters, the global air freight and logistics company within the Industrials sector is a macro bellwether. CEO Raj Subramaniam’s comments on the call will offer the latest takes on the state of economic activity.

From there, the April 8 FedEx Freight Investor Day 2026 could set up the rest of the calendar year. The gathering comes with FDX shares sliding after a torrid rally from $190 last April to $391 in late February.

Higher oil prices are an immediate profitability threat, while geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East do the shipper no favors either. FedEx is also among the many companies seeking refunds from President Trump’s IEEPA tariffs that were struck down by the Supreme Court last month. With so many crosscurrents, keep this Analyst Day on your radar.

The Bottom Line

Analyst Days offer clues on company-specific trends and strategy as well as indications of macroeconomic health. AI, data centers, the consumer, and overall business activity are in focus from the aforementioned five company events on tap. The aggregate tone will lay the foundation for the Q1 reporting season, which kicks off in less than a month.

Original Post

data.title: YBTC: Bitcoin Covered Call Strategy May Outperform Bitcoin, But Risks A Market Correction
data.description:
YBTC SA Repot 3.18.26

The Roundhill Bitcoin Covered Call Strategy ETF (YBTC) is an alternative income exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with indirect exposure to bitcoin through a synthetic covered call strategy. While covered call strategies tend to limit the full upside potential of the fund given the short call position, the market may be positioned for YBTC to outperform bitcoin, creating a differentiated investment opportunity for those seeking exposure to the performance of bitcoin while earning income through a derivatives strategy. While I believe YBTC may outperform bitcoin in the near future as a result of the premium earned on sold call options, bitcoin may be entering the final corrective wave when applying Elliott Wave Theory, suggesting that the price of bitcoin may continue its decline going forward. As a result of this, I am recommending YBTC with a Sell rating.

Investment Thesis for YBTC

YBTC employs a synthetic covered call strategy to provide investors with exposure to the performance of bitcoin. A synthetic covered call strategy is similar to a covered call strategy with respect to taking both a long and short position with respect to the underlying asset; the core difference between the two types of strategies is that a synthetic covered call strategy is entirely made up of derivatives whereas a covered call strategy has direct ownership of the underlying assets.

In the case of YBTC, the fund provides indirect exposure to bitcoin by taking both long and short positions in options derivatives, though has the flexibility to take a direct position in a bitcoin ETF per the fund’s prospectus. The fund does this by buying call options and selling put options to create synthetic long exposure to bitcoin. In turn, the fund will sell out-of-the-money call options in order to earn premium for the fund. While this can be an appealing investment strategy when the price of bitcoin trades within a certain range during the options’ holding period, the fund may limit the full upside potential as a result of the covered call options sold while exposing investors to the full downside risk during a market correction.

On the basis of these factors, I have reason to believe YBTC could outperform bitcoin in the near future following a series of repositionings that occurred in late-2025 and early-2026.

Towards the end of 2025, bitcoin underwent a major correction that led to the liquidation of over $19b in leveraged positions within a 24-hour period. These events were the precursor to a further price correction with bitcoin declining to a 1-year+ low of $62.1k per coin in February 2025 before modestly recovering.

While I believe the liquidation of leveraged positions was the beginning of the price correction, I suspect a large component of the deeper decline was the result of the institutionalization of bitcoin, adding bitcoin exposure through the use of spot ETFs in portfolios of individual investors that may not be as comfortable with the volatility bitcoin presents with respect to traditional equity and fixed income funds. Bitcoin spot ETFs were established in January 2024 following the approval by the SEC, leading to billions of fund inflows into these bitcoin spot ETFs. With the emergence of these funds came the emergence of risk-averse investors seeking to participate in the returns of a risky asset.

In addition to this, with the mass adoption of bitcoin ETFs, liquidity was fundamentally baked into the ETFs, providing investors with a highly liquid asset that can be converted into cash with minimal spread risk. I suspect that the initial price decline experienced in October 2025 led to the widespread exodus from bitcoin spot ETFs and potentially the repositioning out of less liquid funds and into funds with greater assets under management.

You may recall that the market exhibited euphoria in 2025 with the passage of the GENIUS Act and a number of other legislation being considered to provide safeguards for cryptocurrency as well as the establishment of the strategic bitcoin reserve. Despite the political support for cryptocurrencies, the market remains as the ultimate price-setting mechanism for the coins, determining the price of bitcoin based solely on supply and demand.

Looking ahead, I believe bitcoin exposure has been in a state of normalization following said period of euphoria. While the price decline may continue in the future, or reverse, I suspect that the steps laid out above, inclusive of the liquidation of highly leveraged accounts and ETF fund outflows, are suggestive that the greater risk to price volatility has been reduced. This could eventually result in price stability, providing an ideal environment for YBTC to perform its best.

Looking at the technical chart, I have reason to believe that the price decline of bitcoin is entering the final wave down in the retracement cycle when applying Elliott Wave Theory. Elliott Wave Theory is based on the Fibonacci Sequence, measuring the market through a wave pattern with 5 wave cycles up followed by 3 wave cycles down. This may mean that the final corrective wave is imminent.

The completion of the final downcycle may result in a reversal or a continuation of the downcycle, which may be dependent on investor sentiment and potential exogenous factors that may occur in the future.

Given the potential for the 3rd downcycle, I believe YBTC may face downward pressure, though it has the potential to outperform bitcoin on a total return basis. While the fund exhibits full downside exposure through the synthetic long position, YBTC will continue to earn premium from the sale of out-of-the-money call options, generating incremental value.

About Roundhill Bitcoin Covered Call Strategy ETF

YBTC was launched by Roundhill on January 18, 2024 on the Cboe BZX Exchange. The strategy has a gross expense ratio of 96bps, a relatively high fee when compared to peer bitcoin covered call strategies.

YBTC has roughly $171mm in net assets with an average of $4.33mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing moderate liquidity for investors seeking to trade the fund.

YBTC pays out weekly distributions with an annualized rate of $17.66/share over the last twelve months, yielding 76.31%.

When evaluating these types of funds, investors should review both total returns and price returns to fully understand the value earned from ownership. Per the fund’s most recent 19a-1 notice, 100% of the distribution was derived from return of capital. ROC provides investors with a tax-deferred benefit that lowers the cost basis of the investment. Once the cost basis reaches $0/share, excess ROC will be taxed as capital gains. The benefit of the fund’s price decline is that the future price at which the fund may be sold can potentially narrow the gap, though will also influence the actual return the investor earns from owning the fund. As part of this, investors will be faced with NAV erosion that may deteriorate the value of their investment over time.

Risks Related to YBTC

YBTC provides investors with exposure to a synthetic covered call strategy, presenting certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision.

Synthetic covered call strategies may limit the full upside potential for an investor while exposing them to the full downside risk of the holdings. The strategy is dependent on market liquidity and potential volatility to earn premium on options sold; greater volatility in the underlying asset may result in higher premium earned. Tighter liquidity in the options market that the fund participates in may result in uneven options pricing. As a result of the underlying strategy, investors may be exposed to NAV erosion over the course of their holding period. Investing in YBTC is not a direct investment in bitcoin.

Final Thoughts

With bitcoin undergoing a potential corrective wave, YBTC may be positioned to outperform bitcoin, though may face downside exposure resulting from its long bitcoin call and short bitcoin put options positions. As a result of this, I am recommending YBTC with a Sell rating.

data.title: Palantir: Probably The Most Consequential AI Company In Our Dangerous World
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) stock is not for the faint of heart; it's that simple. PLTR is arguably one of my highest conviction software stocks right now. Amid the AI-eating-software narrative that also resulted in PLTR falling by 40% from its November 2025 highs, this was also the worst hammering investors had to endure before the stock mounted a recovery over the past few weeks. In my previous Palantir write-up (before the SaaS capitulation), I argued that the company looked to have strengthened its moat and value proposition after its Q4 earnings, but then, it got engulfed in the software forest fire that spread wildly.

I guess if you have been reading about the SaaS apocalypse, since it has been well covered by the media, I wouldn't go into the specifics in this article. But a critical takeaway that I hope you could get from my update is that PLTR’s nascent recovery hasn’t only seen it outperform the S&P 500 recently but also saw its performance overtake that of its software peers. While the stock remains well below the November highs at this point, I will argue that this is the moment that we must lean in further, taking advantage of the dislocation, even though I acknowledge that the high valuations could still deter some of you from participating. That's okay, but at least you have got to understand what drives the bullish narrative behind my conviction.

Valuation has always been one of my key anchor points when I assess a stock. And it's not often that I hold up the placard telling you that you should consider Palantir as one of the best software plays in town right now, despite posting a forward earnings multiple of more than 100x. Just from that, I believe you will agree with me that it requires not only a fundamentally strong narrative, but also flawless execution. The market will not hold back any punches if there's any semblance of weakness from sales growth, or even from potentially seeing Palantir being replaced by the AI companies that want in on its business. No doubt, the risks are rising with the ascendence of Claude Code, but it’s far from straightforward. Because if there's one company that has made ontology work in the real world and at enterprise scale and proven military grade, creating that incredible orchestration layer across disparate and often not seamlessly integrated enterprise SaaS applications, I believe it's none other than Palantir, which is why its narrative is still so understated.

I stumbled upon an interesting article on The Information that "traditional enterprise apps don’t love the idea of being treated as merely a source of data." Exactly right, right? The so-called system of record is now under attack by software upstarts using Claude Code to design new applications to circumvent the legacy systems and threaten their value proposition and thus derate their moat and their pricing strategies.

Now, these companies are forced to contemplate whether their seat-based model is still relevant or whether they may need to encompass other pricing strategies that include consumption-based models, which are clearly not the bread and butter for SaaS companies that have familiarized themselves with the high-margin per-seat subscription model. And I guess this noticeably led to a rupture in market sentiment, which was also felt in PLTR as well, given the sharp downside volatility until recently.

However, the point about Palantir being so incredibly robust was demonstrated in the recent fracas between the Department of War and Anthropic (ANTHRO). As the DoW labels the American AI company as a "supply chain risk," it has also brought the feud to an unprecedented level, as such a labeling was previously used only for companies from potential adversaries. While the lawsuit and legal tussle between the government and Anthropic are going on, I believe those of us who have witnessed the kerfuffle would also have understood the criticality of the orchestration layer that Palantir plays in between them. Palantir uses Anthropic’s AI models in the customized systems that it designs for the DoW and its agencies for military uses. The decision making layer, the data integration layer, and making sense of the multitudinous data sources (the multi-domain AI for lightning quick targeting decisions... it's all about decisions!) to make actionable decisions are what Palantir excels in, and it is clearly demonstrated in this confrontation.

This is precisely why Palantir’s thesis has such a bulletproof defense that is so difficult, in my opinion, for these AI LLM pure plays to try and unhinge PLTR from their customers’ ecosystems and transfer the value that they desire. More so, especially since these LLM companies are still trying to get into the profitability zone, which is unlikely through the end of the decade, even as they attempt to broaden their sales channels into the enterprise sphere.

For PLTR, because of its proven capabilities that its customers obviously value greatly, they could rely on the company to help them decide which of the AI models are the most appropriate, whether they are proprietary or open source.

This orchestration layer is what the enterprise software firms are leaning deeper into, and that has been what PLTR has always been doing, right? Talk about first mover advantage, as PLTR uses its customized operating systems for their customers to help them create immense value. As argued by Palantir CEO Alex Karp in his short but consequential AIPCon 9 keynote recently, the need for companies is to rely on Palantir more, not less. And what’s more, if companies want to gain even more cutting edge capabilities that could be used to upend their competition, then staying on board with Palantir will be increasingly pivotal, as Karp emphasized:

We do not build software that is parasitic, somehow clients have to keep it when it's no longer useful. Which is dangerous. That's how we ended up innovating and being Maven, and Maven, which is the targeting backbone of modern warfare and many other things that is not in the public space. And quite frankly, a lot of tech innovations until about 20 years ago, in the history of humanity, that it all started in the military context and then brought into the commercial context. You will see a lot of that in our commercial deployments. One of the things that we are trying very hard to do across our many, many, many valuable partnerships, is also migrate you from the product you were using with us two years ago, closer to what the battlefield is using. Meaning, next generation use cases of foundry, making implementations orders of magnitude faster, making them more valuable in ontology. - Alex Karp keynote at Palantir AIPCon 9

Some of you may ask, if that's the case, why Palantir’s estimated revenue for 2026 comes in markedly below what Anthropic or OpenAI has delivered through March on an annualized basis. As seen above, OpenAI is expected to have secured $25B in revenue through early March 2026, while Anthropic has closed in with $19B of its own.

Now here is the thing about Palantir. The company isn’t just in the business of selling software or AI models that may or may not have a significant value proposition for the enterprise customer.

Obviously, Palantir works with enterprise customers (not consumer AI) on driving the use cases, and PLTR's reported participation in the Iranian War through the incredible success of Project Maven could convince investors and customers that real-world battlefield experience will be translated into not just incremental, but possibly transformational commercial gains for customers maximizing those advantages on the Palantir platform.

Which is why the forward-deployed engineers are out there looking very closely with customers to not only customize Palantir’s systems to their organizations’ workflows, but more importantly, to make sure the systems work and create tangible value for their customers, making them willing to spend even more, and thus accruing more value to Palantir over time.

And because of that, Palantir’s orchestration layer isn't just a theoretical setup that customers need to experiment with for some time to understand better. There are already real world proven scenarios in the battlefield and also in the commercial world where Palantir drives significant value.

However, that also suggests that Palantir needs to be more selective with customers at this stage, while it’s still expanding its commercial business rapidly, on how their offerings are perceived as instrumental, and not easily replaceable by perceived or legacy competitors that aren’t likely to have that ontology framework that Palantir has been working with for years now.

Hence, choosing organizations where Palantir has the most significant impact may not expand that immediate market opportunity that quickly, with revenue expected to reach $14.9B by 2028, as compared to the $25B that OpenAI has already garnered through early March, but the real-world value proposition will also help keep free cash flow margins at an incredibly high level, at the 50% mark or even above. In other words, if you want a well-established and validated business case that can sustain itself, Palantir’s narrative is right up there for us to consider.

And, for PLTR, in all honesty, I couldn't have been more satisfied with the print. It took more than three months for the stock to finally find its footing across undulating terrain that culminated in the selloff in February as SaaS investors bailed out quickly as the AI eats software fears percolated across the entire market.

Yet, PLTR has since regained control of the decisive $140 level, and is also starting to outperform peers again, despite still being valued at a forward earnings multiple of 115x. Yet, it is also well below the 3Y average of 122x, which might give some of PLTR bulls the signal to return and accumulate while the market was less optimistic.

And with PLTR seemingly holding on to that bottom well, I think it sets the stage for the stock to continue its ascent back toward those highs that it notched in 2025. If you have been waiting for such an opportunity to add, I will urge you not to miss the current opportunity.

Rating: Upgrade to Strong Buy.

data.title: 2 Deep Value BDCs For True Contrarian Investors
data.description:
The BDC sector (BIZD) is oversold.

Almost all BDCs trade below their net asset values and offer 10%+ dividend yield opportunities.

Among these opportunities we also can find high-quality names, which have been thrown out with the bathwater (irrespective of their SaaS exposures, non-accrual status, NAV dynamics etc.).

Given that the sector median discount is 28% and many high-quality names have indeed been punished so hard, I don't think that there is a huge merit of taking extra risk (or above average risk).

For example, we can buy defensive and resilient BDCs such as Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC) and Ares Capital (ARCC) while still capturing discounted loan books and 10%+ yields.

Having said that I could see the logic of at least taking a look and potentially dipping toes (in a safe fashion) in what I would call "deep value zone" - e.g., the P/NAV bottom quartile BDCs.

The risk level obviously is higher there, but so is the return potential.

Granted, this kind of move could result in two very different outcomes.

Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) and BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC) are great examples of how deep value investing could play out in a negative way (see my article here). TriplePoint Venture Growth (NYSE:TPVG) is another "bottom quartile" case, which has destroyed a significant chunk of shareholder value. In this area we also can find FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), which has disappointed me personally as well - see article here.

However, just because there are a bunch of real-life bad outcome examples, it does not mean that everything that is in the deep value zone is garbage (at least if assessed on a risk adjusted basis).

Now, going back to what safely dipping toes in the deep value zone actually means:

  • Opening relatively small positions.
  • Having a high conviction case that the discount is primarily driven by emotional or immaterial to long-term value creation factors rather than struggling fundamentals.

With this in mind, let me elaborate on two heavily discounted BDCs, where the risks are indeed higher, but the incremental upside potential is far greater.

Currently, these two BDCs are guilty until they are proven innocent. My assumption is that it could take anywhere from 2 (until next 10-Q reports) to 12 months for these investment theses to play out.

#1: OTF, 34% discount, 11.1% yield

Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF) is a relatively fresh BDC, which had tapped the IPO market back in June, 2025.

Just as the sector behemoth Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), OTF is related to Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), which has not received the best press lately (i.e., failed attempt to merge OBDC with OBDC II and liquidity gating to stabilize liquidity outflows).

The link to OWL has most probably been one of the reasons behind the massive discount.

Another driver for the discount is what is associated with the post-IPO lock-ups, where OTF gradually releases additional shares to the market (its free-float). This adds additional supply of shares (each month until June 2026).

Boosting supply while many BDC investors are fleeing (i.e., selling) is probably not that positive for the share price.

Finally, the fact that OTF has about 70% of portfolio placed in software companies does not help either amid the chatter around SaaSacalopyses.

Here is how OTF has performed since the IPO:

In my view, all the aforementioned discount drivers have nothing to do with the actual reality and the underlying fundamentals.

For example, OTF ended Q4 2025 ended with a non-accrual of 0.2% from the total portfolio FV, which is one of the lowest levels in the entire BDC space.

In fact, OTF has been one of the few BDCs out there that has reported a positive NAV growth dynamic in Q4 - i.e., the NAV per share increased by $0.18 from positive unrealized and realized gains.

This dynamic really goes hand in hand with the following:

  • OTF's weighted average portfolio company net LTV is 34%.
  • The weighted average portfolio company EBITDA is $260 million.
  • More than 80% of loans are first-lien.
  • The portfolio includes several lucrative equity/warrant positions in such names SpaceX and Revolut.

These are some strong credit risk statistics.

On top of that, OTF has a conservative capital structure of 0.75x, which is way below the target range of 1.00x - 1.25x.

From this level OTF can go on offense now when many private BDCs are forced to deal with liquidity issues, which is inherently accommodative to higher spreads.

Alternatively, OTF can simply put heavier emphasis on its authorized share repurchase program of up to $300 million.

Most BDCs do not have this kind of luxury since their debt profiles already are largely exhausted - i.e., sector average debt to equity is 1.25x.

In my view, OTF is a de-risked deep value play with much greater upside potential than further downside risk.

#2: CION, 50% discount, 17.8% yield

The reason why CION Investment Corporation (CION) is so discounted is largely attributable to two items:

  • PIK-heavy income profile.
  • Material unrealized losses.

Based on the P/NAV of ~0.50x we can conclude that the market is telling us that the risks here are much higher than for OTF.

CION has no ties to OWL, no headwinds from lock-up releases, almost no exposure to SaaS.

Instead, it is primarily all about the two aforementioned items, which, as we can see in the chart below, is something that really concerns the market:

The chart clearly shows how just recently CION's share price took a notable beating. It coincides exactly with the publication of Q4 2025 earnings report in which the BDC reported a notable unrealized NAV loss.

The NAV per share dropped by 7.4% on a quarter-to-quarter basis, which does not look good.

However, if we peel back the onion a bit, we will recognize that the main item, which drove down the NAV metric was the unrealized mark-to-market adjustments on certain equity stakes, which do not contribute to the NII per share generation.

Instead, these markdowns have been made to integrate a more prudent view on the potential equity valuations, which go hand in hand with the depressed sentiment in the economy. Namely, nothing has stemmed from permanent write-offs or bankruptcies.

In the meantime, the core debt (income producing) portfolio remains in a solid shape.

For instance, in Q4 the weighted average interest coverage across CION's portfolio companies increased from 1.94x to 2.26x. Portfolio-wide EBITDA growth also was positive. The non-accrual level landed at 1.78% of the total portfolio FV, which is still below the sector average of around 2.5%.

As I mentioned above, CION has insignificant exposure to SaaS - i.e., 1.8% of the portfolio at FV. Plus, there are no ARR loans in place, which means that all loan investments have been underpinned by positive EBITDA generation levels.

Now, the big question here is about PIK, which accounts for ~20% of the total income. It is one of the highest levels in the sector (competing with TPVG).

Yet, the devil lies in the details:

  • ~73% of PIK investments are associated with risk rating 1 or 2 companies (performing as expected or better).
  • ~99% stem from risk rating 1 - 3 companies, where "rating 3" means that there have been some slight deviations from the base expectations, but still there are no credit struggles present (or with a high probability of happening).

In a nutshell, the market has assumed that about half of CION's portfolio is worthless, while the underlying performance remains in good shape.

Sure, the PIK component introduces higher risks, but, in my humble opinion, these risks are not commensurate with the current discount (e.g., PIK accounts for 20% of the total income, while the discount is 50%, which already indicates some kind of a disconnect).

Just as for OTF, I see strong upside potential, which more than offsets incremental risks (mainly because of the massive discount, which provides significant room for an error).

data.title: SoundHound AI: Moats Are Resonating, But The Premium Is Deafening (Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This would mark the third time I’m covering SoundHound AI, Inc. (SOUN), one of those companies trying to make talking to machines feel natural, like talking to a person instead of pressing buttons or yelling at an electronic-sounding voice assistant. Basically, they build software that lets businesses add really smart voice assistants into their products like in drive-throughts, cars, and customer service lines.

Last year when I covered the stock, in terms of market sentiment, we had a lot of animal spirits and FOMO, which tends to be a red flag for me for investments like this. Plus, to be honest, while the tech itself seemed impressive, it reminded me of a product like GoPro, Inc. (GPRO), which came out of the gates super hot a little more than a decade ago, but when you strip away the curtain, it was just a convenient camera wrapped in a protective casing. In other words, it was a product that you could easily clone or replicate in various forms. And look at GPRO today, it’s a penny stock, and that was really my thought process with SOUN, which, by the looks of it, has been graced by Seeking Alpha’s red label “high risk” warning.

In hindsight, I gave the momentum crowd too much credit; after my last Hold call, the stock had a brief pop, then settled into a steady grind down. Today, after news of the CFO’s departure, I reviewed the latest numbers and moved from neutral to bearish, as the trade now is mostly risk, with the margin for error wiped out.

SoundHound AI, Inc. Growth Drivers and Financial Position

  • FY 2025 Revenue: $169M (+99% YoY)
  • Q4 2025 Revenue: $55.1M (+59% YoY).

Over the entire year, SoundHound’s total revenue nearly doubled compared to the previous year. In the fourth quarter alone, revenue increased by 59%. And the gains didn’t come from a single hot streak but from new business landing in multiple places at once. The company says enterprise AI is where things are clicking most, particularly in healthcare and finance. In these industries, businesses are dealing with higher labor costs and more complicated customer service needs.

  • Automotive MAU Growth: 50%+ YoY
  • Automotive Query Growth: ~75% YoY.

The automotive part also continued to grow, especially in faster-growing Asian markets. New agreements and renewed contracts with OEMs tells us voice tech is becoming harder to ignore in cars. Personally, and I only bring this up because I'm not alone in this thought process, I'm more than happy driving my now vintage Dodge Ram that doesn't talk nor track my movements. But I digress.

  • Restaurant Orders (Q4): 9M+ calls.

Beyond the dashboard, better-connected systems gave restaurants a lift, with more orders coming in. At the same time, voice-based shopping started to show early signs of momentum, linking cars, televisions, and businesses together to make buying things easier and more seamless. Keep in mind, no single customer made up more than 10% of total revenue. It's not uncommon to come across smaller AI companies which often depend heavily on just one or two major clients, which can be risky. Fewer dependencies, more stability, that’s what spreading revenue across customers buys them, along with time.

  • Q4 GAAP Gross Margin: 48%
  • Q4 Non-GAAP Gross Margin: 61%.

Meanwhile, efficiency looks like it's also on the upswing. SoundHound's gross margins improved because it upgraded its infrastructure, reduced cloud costs, combined older systems, and intentionally moved away from lower-quality contracts. At the same time, the company still chose to invest in certain deals that have strong potential for automation in the future. Profits dip in the short run, but the company is betting on a longer-term return.

  • R&D Expense: $24.8M (+22% YoY)
  • Sales & Marketing Expense: $17.4M (+82% YoY)
  • G&A Expense: $21.2M (+29% YoY)
  • Adjusted EBITDA: -$7.4M (improved 56% YoY)
  • Cash & Equivalents: $248M
  • Debt: $0.

Still, operating costs went up, mainly because the company made acquisitions, which added more employees and strengthened its development capabilities. The company also increased its sales and marketing efforts. Even so, the adjusted EBITDA loss got smaller. The company also has no debt and a solid amount of cash, so it’s not under any immediate financial pressure.

  • Monthly Query Volume: billions/month (12x since IPO).

As I noted earlier, the tech looks highly replicable to me and open to disruption. Naturally, management sees it the other way, and pointed to the results as proof of what they say are their technical advantages. Their moat, if you will. They've built up decades of conversational data, use their own models that they claim are more accurate and cheaper than those from big tech companies, and rely on a system that combines set rules, generative AI, and human support.

As CEO Keyvan Mohajer put it,

“we believe we are entering a new era where companies with deep tech and data moats will create the most value,” adding that SoundHound “has a massive amount of data and has processed billions of interactions over the years across all major global languages,” which they argue gives them scale advantages that “new players are faced with” and “we’ve long since overcome.”

He said what separates them is owning the stack end to end and dialing it in, adding,

“we have our own models that we’ve created over a long period of time with a lot of data and we compare it with the big tech models and we beat them in accuracy, speed and cost,”

but not so rigid that they can’t pull in outside models when required.

At the platform level, he made the case that the advantage is structural: “our Agentic platform is the only solution that is ready to be deployed across a multitude of vertical use cases… all with a unified AI agent framework,” allowing customers to “build an agent once and deploy it anywhere,” and combining “agentic, deterministic, and human-assisted understanding” into a single system.

That said, it’s important to stay realistic. Voice AI is a very competitive space. Big tech companies provide the core technology, while many newer companies are competing for their share of the market. SoundHound does have an edge, though. Its deep expertise in specific industries like hospitality and automotive gives it an advantage that more general, one-size-fits-all competitors don’t have.

However, there are also some concerns. The company just announced that its CFO will leave in early April, and co-founder James Hom will step in as interim CFO. This creates a period of transition. Managing finances and deciding how to invest money are especially critical during times of rapid growth. Even if the change goes smoothly, it can still distract attention from other priorities.

SoundHound AI: Valuation on Full Volume

Speaking of priorities, let's look at SOUN's valuation. Clearly, investors are paying a massive premium compared to others in the same sector. The EV to sales ratio is 18.13x based on past performance and 13.15x based on expected future performance. In comparison, the typical company in the sector has ratios of 3.26 and 2.97.

The pattern repeats in other metrics. The price-to-sales ratio is 18.77 based on past results and 14.20 based on expected future results, compared to typical sector levels of 3.20 and 3.01. Meanwhile, the price-to-book ratio is 7.13, while the sector average is 3.55.

Cut through it, and the market's pricing in outsized growth. The smoking gun most likely comes from very strong growth numbers: revenue has increased by 99.45% year-over-year, and is expected to grow another 48.93% going forward. This is far higher than the usual sector growth rate of around 10%.

However, the company's still losing a lot of money, so usual profit measures don’t really tell us much.

It's also constantly spending more cash than it brings in. On top of that, the same old issues are still there. Losses, dilution, and a reliance on external funding haven’t gone away.

What you’re looking at here is the share count exploding over time, climbing from barely 12 million a few years ago to over 400 million today, which is the clearest sign that the company has been funding its growth by issuing more and more stock.

Final Verdict

Rating: Sell. The downside risk is much bigger than the potential upside. SOUN's trading at very high valuation levels while still losing money and burning through cash. The company's growing quickly, and that growth is real. The catch is the stock already reflects a best-case scenario playing out over years.

One final note, a possibility I’ve been seeing making the rounds, is that SOUN could absolutely become a more attractive takeover target if the share price falls further. I haven’t dug into the options chain yet, but at the right price, a strangle could make sense with that bet in mind.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHD: Winning The Rotation Trade With High-Quality Yield
data.description:
SCHD Still Looks Good For The Capital Rotation Trade

In my previous article on Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) that came out in mid-September 2025, I expected that the extreme tech valuations at the time would eventually lead to a capital rotation to dividend-paying stable stocks, like most of the names within SCHD's holdings. That was the reason for my core expectation of SCHD likely beating the S&P 500 index (SP500), at least in the short term. And so it happened - SCHD has beaten SPY since September 2025 as I expected:

I believe that the core structure of the fund - the combo of high-quality dividend payers and low expense ratio - makes SCHD a nice pick for the current macro environment when we're talking about capital preservation and risk-focused growth. I wouldn't expect massive, extraordinary returns for value/GARP investors from SCHD, but it's a stable ETF whose strategy keeps working as expected, and even major geopolitical risk-offs can't shake it as much as some of its peers. The capital rotation trade doesn't seem to be over (at least for now), so I keep my Buy on SCHD in place.

Why Do I Think So?

If you've read my most recent articles on big tech firms, you probably know that I'm bullish on Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and most other mega-cap AI enablers because I believe most of them won't lose the moat that helped them increase their market cap and valuation over the past few decades. But even as a bull, I can't cancel out the fact that these companies spend their FCFs like crazy:

While I believe most of those FCFs will eventually translate into tangible returns, I agree that there's a risk that a) it can take a lot of time, and b) it may not be realized in full. If these risks look more likely to you than to me, and your risk aversion doesn't let you accept them, then buying SPY alone doesn't really make sense - the tech exposure there is still high despite the fact that the MAG 7 (MAGS) is down almost 9% YTD at the time of this writing.

Capital is now clearly rotating from mega caps that burn cash on AI initiatives to less capitalized companies and names that are unlikely to be disrupted much by AI, and are able to keep their FCF generation capacity in solid shape. This is where SCHD comes in with its exposure to Energy, where FCF generation benefits massively from the geopolitical crisis in the Middle East (and generally in the world). But even if oil prices crater tomorrow, the biggest sectoral exposure of SCHD at less than 22% (Energy exposure) wouldn't let it lose that much, in my opinion.

The top 10 holdings within SCHD's portfolio account for <43%, with the biggest 3 positions accounting for 4.82%, 4.32%, and 4.31%, respectively. The smallest top-10 position is 4.01% - it's PepsiCo's (PEP) ordinary shares. How does it compare to SPY? Well, SPY's top-10 accounts for only 36.35%, which looks better at first glance. But the biggest 3 positions account for 7.31%, 6.63%, and 4.96%, while the smallest on the top-10 list is only 1.57%. You see - the composition is clearly skewed, and if tech underperforms against energy or industrials, then SCHD is likely to keep beating SPY again and again in the next few months.

There's a catch here - you're buying SCHD for rising dividends, while SPY is used for a mix of dividends and growth (and, of course, the constituents' size). I've taken SCHD's top ten holdings and took what's important in this regard - the next year's yields and the n+1 growth in dividend payments (i.e., the 2028 growth in most cases). Also, I've added the Seeking Alpha Quant's Dividend Safety Score to see how the quantitative system views the companies' dividend safety (this metric basically removes my possible bias).

# Name Dividend Yield - 2027 Dividend growth in 2028 Dividend safety, SA Quant
1 Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) 2.28% 6.20% A+
2 ConocoPhillips (COP) 2.84% 2.85% A-
3 Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) 5.78% 1.74% C+
4 Chevron Corporation (CVX) 3.74% 4.57% B
5 Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) 4.38% 0.77% B-
6 Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) 3.04% 5.75% A-
7 Altria Group, Inc. (MO) 6.89% 4.01% B-
8 Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) 3.12% 4.87% A
9 The Coca-Cola Company (KO) 2.94% 4.91% B-
10 PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) 4.02% 5.02% A-

Source: Seeking Alpha data, compiled by the author.

The average yield among the top ten is ~3.9%, while the payouts are projected to rise by over 4% (on average) in 2028. All this looks very good, in my opinion. If we take SCHD's peer like Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), we'd see that a) its holdings structure favors tech, hence it's risky under the assumption of the ongoing capital rotation, and b) the yields are much lower as most of the companies prefer buybacks over dividends.

Another peer - iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) - looks better diversified as its top-10 accounts for 26.4% of the whole fund. But when we pick the constituents individually, we're not getting the same ~4% growth in 2028 dividends - DGRO's overdiversification lowers the dividend growth factor, which is why its TTM dividend yield is only 2.08% (SCHD's TTM dividend yield is 3.38%, which makes a difference when we assume millions of dollars invested).

In addition to that, SCHD's expense ratio of only 0.06% looks reasonable versus its peers, in my view, given the above forward dividend growth superiority.

The Bottom Line

As I wrote in my September article on SCHD, I agree that I may be wrong and premature in my judgments regarding the continuation of the current rotation cycle from aggressive assets like SPY - and especially NASDAQ 100 (QQQ) - to defensive ETFs like SCHD that can show more predictable total returns in today's market environment. There's a huge difference between today's market and what we saw in 1999 or 2008, when this kind of rotation was taking a lot of time, because today's fundamentals of the biggest companies within SPY seem to largely stomach the pain of the current CAPEX cycle in the next few years.

Despite the above risks to my bullish thesis on SCHD, I believe that this ETF still looks like a great place for capital reallocation in case the market decides to put the eggs into a safer basket. It's already happening - SCHD has beaten the SPY since calendar Q3 2025. But it looks like just the beginning because a) big tech is unlikely to stop its spending (hence, the risks of low-ROIC will stay elevated), and b) SCHD's dividend growth looks attractive if we assume the rate cuts are on the horizon. Again, I'm a believer that Amazon, Microsoft, and Google will survive the CAPEX cycle, and they'll be hugely profitable in the future. But SCHD looks like a good place to be at the moment if you're not willing to take the tech risks, in my opinion. I keep my Buy on SCHD in place.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Is There An ETF Alternative To Private Markets?
data.description:
Hybrid public-private exchange-traded funds (ETFs) aim to provide exposure to select private companies while leveraging the liquidity and transparency of public markets.

But what if investors aren’t actually realizing the upside they expect from those private companies, and/or are underperforming in the public portion of their portfolio because of how each component is structured?

If the private portion of a hybrid ETF isn’t structured effectively, investors may not obtain the exposure characteristics they expect from private market investments. It’s also important to remember that at least 85% of a hybrid ETF’s portfolio is typically invested in liquid public markets.1 When that portion is not constructed effectively, it can potentially affect the fund’s overall performance in unintended ways.

Avoiding Negative Surprises in Hybrid Portfolios

Some hybrid ETFs have struggled to deliver on expectations and have led some people to question the concept. But we believe the idea of combining public and private market exposure remains sound when executed thoughtfully.

If your goal is exposure to specific private companies rather than an opaque, pooled structure, it can be far more effective to participate directly on that company’s capitalization table rather than through a layered vehicle such as a special purpose vehicle (SPV). Direct exposure provides clearer ownership rights, potentially lower fees, and more transparent alignment with the underlying company’s performance.

In comparison, some hybrid structures may have limited transparency, added complexity, and additional costs that make it challenging for investors to clearly assess where value creation is occurring. Understanding how private exposure is structured and how it contributes to a portfolio is key when evaluating the role such investments are intended to play, particularly given their lower liquidity.

The Importance of the Public Side

Even for hybrid ETFs with private market allocations, the majority of the portfolio remains in publicly traded securities. This makes portfolio construction on the public side crucial. Chasing the “hot” private opportunities while neglecting the broader public equity exposure can lead to uneven outcomes.

That’s where the experience of a skilled fund manager or sub-advisor matters, one who can manage diversified* portfolios and apply risk management processes across different market environments.

Beyond Tech: The Broader Private Equity Lens

Some hybrid ETFs focus narrowly on sectors such as software, AI, or other innovation-driven themes. Yet private equity (PE) as an asset class provides exposure to far more than technology; it includes industrials, healthcare, consumer, and other sectors that provide diversification* and resilience across market cycles.

While some of the software and technology companies that have been selling off in late 2025 and early 2026 may eventually rebound, the recent sector volatility has highlighted the importance of avoiding overconcentration and building balanced exposure across industries.

KraneShares offers two ETFs that provide exposure to private-market investment characteristics and select private companies through liquid ETF structures:

Access Private Equity-Related Investment Drivers With BUYO

For investors seeking exposure to the characteristics of companies targeted by PE, rather than direct ownership of specific private companies, there are now liquid strategies available.

These approaches use quantitative models to identify and mimic the exposures that have historically been observed in private equity investing. KraneShares partnered with Man Group, the world's largest publicly traded hedge fund manager and an alternative investments specialist, to launch the KraneShares Man Buyout Beta Index ETF (BUYO).2 BUYO seeks to provide exposure to selected PE return drivers through a liquid, transparent ETF structure. This method can be thought of as a “smart beta” approach for PE, providing access to PE-like risk premia within a public market vehicle.

Just as ETFs exist to track broad asset classes like U.S. large-cap equities, emerging markets, or fixed income segments, investors can now access selected PE return drivers through a liquid ETF structure.

Access Select Private Companies With AGIX

For investors seeking direct ownership of select private innovators, including Anthropic and SpaceX, as well as a public equity sleeve with risk controls and index-based construction, KraneShares has a potential solution.

KraneShares partnered with Etna Capital Management, founded by AI-native engineers and investors that are embedded in today's AI ecosystem, to launch the KraneShares Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AGIX). Since its inception on 7/17/2024, AGIX has delivered notable outperformance relative to the Nasdaq 100 Index, which is a major technology and AI benchmark, returning +39.09% versus 27.54% over the same period.3 Additionally, AGIX's lower overlap with crowded mega-cap tech and focus on emerging AI companies across hardware, infrastructure, and applications is highlighted by having just a 50.20% holdings overlap with the Nasdaq-100 Index.4

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed or sold, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted.

By systematically targeting companies with Etna's proprietary AI Exposure Score, AGIX has identified AI-related opportunities that may be underrepresented in broad tech indices such as the Nasdaq-100 Index. Importantly, AGIX’s private market sleeve is designed to complement, not overshadow, its public equity core, which remains the majority of the portfolio.

Just as investors can now access characteristics of companies targeted by PE through BUYO — AGIX offers a differentiated approach to marrying public and private markets through a liquid, exchange-traded format.

Conclusion

Hybrid ETFs hold promise but also pitfalls, particularly when their private allocations are structured through SPVs or when the public side fails to complement the total portfolio exposure. We believe investors should remain mindful that these structures are complex and require disciplined design to deliver the intended exposure and portfolio characteristics.

BUYO seeks to offer a broad-based, fully liquid avenue that provides exposure to a subset of public equities that feature the key characteristics of companies historically held in PE/buyout funds. Alternatively, AGIX takes a more concentrated approach, blending public and private investments tied to the AI ecosystem. For investors seeking exposure to private market characteristics or select private companies within a liquid, publicly traded ETF wrapper, these strategies offer two distinct yet complementary approaches.

*Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss. AGIX and BUYO are non-diversified. The statement regarding diversification refers to sector exposure, not regulatory diversification under the Investment Company Act of 1940.

Citations:

  1. Regulatory Cap: SEC Rule 22e-4 restricts open-end funds and ETFs to holding a maximum of 15% of their net assets in illiquid investments. Target Assets: The 15% illiquid portion often targets private equity, venture capital, or private debt for higher growth potential.
  2. Data from "Managing the World’s Largest Publicly Traded Hedge Fund: Man Group CEO Robyn Grew," Goldman Sachs.
  3. Data from Bloomberg as of 2/28/2026.
  4. Data from Bloomberg as of 12/31/2025.

Index Definitions:

Nasdaq 100 Index: The Nasdaq 100 tracks the performance of 100 of the largest and most actively traded stocks on the Nasdaq exchange, with a technology sector focus.

Solactive Etna Artificial General Intelligence Index: The Solactive Etna Artificial General Intelligence Index is designed to capture the performance of global securities with a high business exposure to the AI business. This includes companies active in the provision of AI Hardware, AI Infrastructure and AI Applications.

Definitions:

Capitalization Table (Cap table): A record of a company’s owners and their ownership percentages, showing how equity is distributed among shareholders and investors.

Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV): A separate legal entity created for a specific investment purpose, often used to hold a single private investment or group of assets.

Quantitative Models: Data-driven mathematical and statistical strategies used to identify patterns or factors that influence investment performance.

Smart Beta: A rules-based investment approach designed to capture specific factors such as value, momentum, or quality rather than simply tracking a market index.

Beta: A measure of how an investment moves relative to the overall market, used to describe its risk and return characteristics.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs International Equity Insights Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI EAFE Index returned 4.86% in the fourth quarter of 2025. This capped a strong year, ending at 31.22%. International stocks outperformed their US counterparts over the quarter and the year. Central banks around the world have taken different paths to cutting rates. The Fed's move toward easing, typically softening the US dollar and boosting overseas revenue translation, has been supportive for certain EAFE constituents. Overall, easing global financial conditions, a strong precious metals market, and geopolitical dynamics continued to drive international equities this past quarter. Additionally, value stocks outperformed growth stocks during the period.

Performance Review

In the fourth quarter of 2025, our International Equity Insights Fund returned 6.16%, outperforming its MSCI EAFE Index benchmark by 130 basis points over the period (net, I-share). All four of our investment pillars contributed to excess returns over the period, as well as country tilts.

Our Themes and Trends investment pillar contributed the most to our excess returns in the fourth quarter. Our suite of signals focusing on industry momentum and economic linkages guided overweight positioning in select diversified Metals & Mining companies, particularly within the UK, Sweden, and Australia. Such names performed strongly in the quarter due to their exposure to precious metals where demand rose amid global tension.

Our Fundamental Mispricings pillar was also additive to performance in the period. Our suite of signals focused on industry rotations guided overweight positioning within specific Diversified Bank companies that outperformed over the period due to strong earnings and resilient profits.

Our High-Quality Business Models pillar was also additive. Select underweights to European software companies contributed to excess returns. Some of these companies struggled amid geopolitical uncertainty as well as a challenging competitive landscape.

Finally, our Sentiment Analysis pillar contributed to performance. Losses stemming from overweight positioning within Aerospace and Defense companies were more than offset by our stock selection within Financials, notably Insurance and Capital Markets companies.

Country Tilts also contributed to performance across the period, although to a lesser extent than our pillars.

Summary Statistics

AUM (All Share Classes, $M) $5,011
Morningstar Category Foreign Large Blend
Inception Date Aug 15, 1997
Number of Holdings 369
Benchmark MSCI EAFE Index
Expense Ratios (Inst'l Share Class), Net/Gross 0.82% / 0.87%

Pillar Contributions To Excess Returns (Gross)

Attribution is relative to the MSCI EAFE Index. Source: MSCI

Past performance does not guarantee future results, which may vary. The value of investments and the income derived from investments will fluctuate and can go down as well as up. A loss of principal may occur

Fundamental Mispricings: We believe that buying high-quality businesses at a fair price leads to strong performance in the long-run

High Quality Business Models: We believe in companies generating high-quality revenues with sustainable business models and aligned management incentives

Market Themes and Trends: Global markets are increasingly theme and trend-driven. We believe that alternative data sources can provide us a lens into trends affecting companies globally, trends that other investors may not be seeing.

Sentiment Analysis: We believe that other market participants can provide valuable information to supplement our own analysis. By analyzing broader market sentiment, we believe we can gain insight into future stock performance.

The MSCI EAFE Index is designed to represent the performance of large and mid-cap securities across 21 developed markets, including countries in Europe, Australasia and the Far East, excluding the U.S. and Canada. data.title: Bridgestone: War-Driven Selloff May Be Creating A Contrarian Buy Opportunity
data.description:
Automotive companies have seen significant share price pressure following the war in the Middle East. One company that has also seen significant pressure on its share price is Bridgestone (BRDCF, BRDCY). In this report, I discuss the company's earnings as well as the outlook, and those already show why the stock price slid 14% since the start of the month. However, I do believe that the valuation could offer a higher-risk, yet compelling, entry point.

Bridgestone Had A Tough Year Navigating Tariffs And Sluggish Demand

The results for 2025 show that the company booked no sales growth. Sales were flat at ¥4.4 trillion. That flat performance was a combination of strategic choices and macro pressures. On one hand, the company focused on premium tires rather than low-margin volume segments, and that already takes out some of the growth. That also aligned with the company's factory closures with exit from weaker markets. On the non-strategic side, the company has been dealing with the impact of sluggish global car production, weak demand in Latin America, and tariffs, which had a ¥25 billion impact. The focus on premium segments and cost rationalization also showed in the 2% growth in adjusted profits and 4% on a constant currency, leading to margins improving from 10.9% to 11.1% and a 40 basis point improvement on constant currency.

In the market for passenger car tires, sales increased 1%, but adjusted operating profit declined 5%, with margins contracting from 11.4% to 10.7%. Given that this segment generates around 56.5% of the company's revenue, low growth or margin erosion has a big impact on the overall business. The segment for bus and truck tires was flat at roughly ¥1 trillion, but profits improved from ¥57.9 billion to ¥88.4 billion. The specialty tire business covering heavy-duty vehicles, airplanes, agricultural equipment, and motorcycles was flat at ¥623.4 billion, with profits dropping 10% to ¥125.5 billion on a weaker business environment in the Americas. Diversified product sales were down 4% to ¥287.1 billion, but margins rose from 4.2% to 11.3%.

In Japan, sales rose 3% and profit increased 6%, while in Asia, sales declined 2%, but profits increased 2%. In the Americas, sales declined 2%, but profit increased 12%, and in EMEA, sales rose 2% and profits increased 42%. So, the company's performance is not necessarily impressive when looking at the growth rates, but in geographical markets and end markets by tire class, the company navigated different dynamics reasonably well.

This Year Could Become Another Challenging Year

For 2026, the company expects 2% growth carried by truck and bus tires as well as replacement tires. The focus on premium tires and cost reduction is expected to drive a 4% increase in profits with margins up 30 basis points. The company expects raw material costs to decline with price, mix, and volume all contributing positively, showing the positive impact of the cost rationalization and focus on higher margin business first before focusing on volume.

The difficult part for Bridgestone is that the macro outlook already is not great, and its guidance shows only small improvement in revenues and a bigger improvement in profits, but it is still relatively small. The situation in the Middle East could erode the growth expectations for the year. Essentially, Bridgestone is a levered play on global mobility. Higher oil prices may deflate the macro growth picture while also pressuring consumer confidence, reducing car purchases and tire replacements. Pressure on global car manufacturing already gave the company a very difficult execution landscape last year.

The spike in oil prices also has an impact on raw material costs. Tires are not just rubber or one sort of rubber. There are various rubbers.

  1. Natural rubber
  2. Synthetic rubbers
    1. Styrene-butadiene rubber
    2. Butadiene rubber
    3. Nitrile Butadiene rubber

Furthermore, there is carbon black to provide strength and durability. The synthetic rubbers and carbon black are derived from petrochemical processes. So, they are oil-linked. Furthermore, logistics costs for raw materials and finished goods also increase as oil prices head higher. So, Bridgestone is likely to see lower demand, higher input costs, and higher logistics costs, and potentially inflation may also come into play.

Bridgestone Could Be a Contrarian Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Bridgestone, I implemented a 4% pressure against prior sales estimates for 2026 and 2027, as well as an 8% pressure on EBITDA due to the loss of leverage and 12% on free cash flow. I do believe that Bridgestone is somewhat more shielded compared to carmakers due to tire replacement needs. Currently, I am not assuming the aforementioned pressures are on a year-on-year basis but a reduction against the in-year estimates. So, if we are looking at year-on-year declines, then the buy case would be shakier.

However, with the pressures implemented, the company would basically see little growth and rely mostly on cost optimization driving 8.5% annual EBITDA growth, while free cash flow was already set to decline as capital expenditures are expected to increase significantly. So, I am marking this a contrarian buy with a $20.34 price target, indicating 10% upside.

While the situation in the Middle East could bring a drag, we note that ongoing cost optimization should still result in significantly higher EBITDA margins compared to 2026. Free cash flow is expected to decline, driven by higher capital intensity, as CapEx as a percentage of sales will grow from 6% to 9%. I also believe that the company does offer a reasonable yield, and it has little debt on the balance sheet relative to its cash balance. This could also allow the company to repurchase shares in times of a weak market environment, which in the process will help the company's dividend sustainability.

Conclusion: A Tough Year Ahead, But Bridgestone Has Some Appeal

I do believe that, given the higher input costs and the uncertain macroeconomic outlook, there is some risk for cyclical companies such as Bridgestone. However, I also believe that the company's cost optimizations can still outshine macroeconomic headwinds. As a result, I am marking the stock a contrarian buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
